content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Learn the concept once, write the syntax four times
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Joan Gamell on UnsplashI used to think learning a new programming language meant starting over. New syntax, new rules, new tutorials, a fresh climb up the same mountain I’d already climbed. So I’d learn one, feel accomplished, and dread the thought of doing it all again for the next.
Then I noticed something while bouncing between Python, JavaScript, Go, and a stubborn weekend of Rust: I wasn’t really relearning programming. I was relearning punctuation. The hard part — understanding what a hash map is, why recursion works, how errors propagate — I only ever had to learn once. Everything after that was translation.
That realization reframes the whole project. You don’t learn four languages. You learn a set of ideas once, and then you spell those ideas out in four different alphabets.
Languages are mostly the same idea wearing different clothes
Strip away the surface and nearly every mainstream language is built from the same handful of concepts: variables and types, control flow, functions, data structures, error handling, and some story about how you organize and abstract code. A conservative estimate is that any two “normal” languages share the overwhelming majority of their conceptual content. What differs is mostly how each one forces you to express it.
Consider the humble key-value store. In Python it’s a dict. In JavaScript it's an object or a Map. In Java it's a HashMap. In Go it's a map. Four names, four syntaxes, one idea. If you already understand what a hash map is and when to reach for one, learning all four spellings is an afternoon of reference-checking, not a week of study.
The trap most learners fall into is treating each language as a separate mountain. They learn “Python dictionaries” as if that’s a Python-specific fact, when really they learned a universal data structure that happens to be named dict in one place.
The mental shift: concept first, syntax second
Here’s the reframe that changes everything. When you sit down to learn something new, don’t ask “How do I do X in Python?” Ask “What is X, really?” — and only then, “How does each language make me say it?”
That second question usually has four short answers. The first question, answered once, unlocks all four.
This is why comparative learning is faster than sequential learning, not slower. It sounds like more work to juggle four languages at once. It’s actually less, because you pay the expensive cost — building the mental model — a single time, and then amortize it across every language you’ll ever touch.
The Rosetta Drill
The practical technique that makes this work is embarrassingly simple. I call it the Rosetta Drill, after the Rosetta Stone that let scholars read one language through another.
For every concept you’re learning:

• Understand it in the abstract. Spend five minutes with the idea itself, divorced from any language. What problem does it solve? When would you use it?

• Write it in your strongest language. Get a working example in the language you’re most comfortable with.

• Translate the exact same program into the others. Same behavior, same inputs, same outputs — just re-expressed.

• Note what changed, and ask why. This step is where the real learning hides.
That fourth step is the whole game. When you translate a program and a language forces you to do something differently, that friction is teaching you the language’s soul.
Write a function that reads a file, and Python lets you shrug off the possibility of failure. Go makes you check an error value on the very next line. Rust refuses to compile until you’ve handled every failure path explicitly. Same task, three philosophies about how much the programmer should be trusted. You don’t learn that from a syntax table — you learn it the moment the compiler yells at you and you’re forced to ask why it cares.
Keep a comparison table (your personal Rosetta Stone)
As you run the drill, build one running document: a table with a row per concept and a column per language. Loops, conditionals, functions, maps, error handling, string formatting — each gets a row, each language gets its cell.
This artifact does two things. It becomes the cheat sheet you’ll actually use when you inevitably forget how Go handles something you last wrote three weeks ago. And the act of filling it in is itself the learning — you can’t complete a cell you don’t understand, so gaps in the table are a precise map of what you don’t yet know.
Choose languages that disagree with each other
There’s a catch. This approach pays off most when your languages actually contrast. Learning four dynamically typed scripting languages teaches you four dialects of the same worldview. You want languages that argue.
A spread that fights productively might be a readable dynamic language to learn concepts fast, an async and event-driven one to feel a different execution model, a statically typed compiled language to force you to think about types and memory, and something functional-leaning to bend your idea of what a program even is. The disagreements between them are the curriculum. Every place they refuse to agree is a place you learn something real about the trade-offs underneath all code.
What you actually walk away with
Let me be honest about the ceiling, because the internet loves to promise mastery in a month and mastery isn’t on the table. Mastery takes years, per language. What this approach gets you is something more useful in the short term: fluency across paradigms. The ability to read code in several languages, write small-to-medium programs in each, understand which philosophy each one represents, and — the real prize — pick up the next language dramatically faster than you picked up the last.
Because here’s the compounding payoff. Once you’ve internalized that languages are ideas in costume, the fifth language isn’t a mountain at all. It’s a translation exercise. You already know the concepts. You’re just learning where this one puts its semicolons, and what it’s opinionated about.
You learned the concepts once. Now you’re just writing the syntax — four times, then five, then however many you like.

If this resonated, try it this week: pick one concept you know well, and write it in three languages you don’t. Watch how little of it is actually new.
